title: Global Implications of China's Rise
gist: A GMF focus-area hub page framing China's economic rise as a costly geopolitical reality that the US and Europe have struggled to adjust to, arguing that competing and defending will require coordinated transatlantic action. The substantive body is a navigation page; the analytic signal is limited to the framing thesis and GMF's current China-watch agenda (South China Sea, the Trump-Xi summit, Europe-China rivalry).

insights.claim: The United States and Europe have struggled to adjust and respond to the new geopolitical reality created by China's growing economic might and influence.
insights.speaker: German Marshall Fund
insights.so_what: Frames the West as reactive rather than proactive on China; watch for whether US-EU policy coordination actually tightens, as fragmentation keeps China-exposure risk mispriced.
insights.quote: the United States and Europe have struggled to adjust and respond to this new geopolitical reality
insights.locator: In a world reshaped by China's growing economic might

insights.claim: Competing with and defending against China's rise will require coordinated action between the US and Europe, because the implications of that rise are proving costly.
insights.speaker: German Marshall Fund
insights.so_what: Signals GMF's policy push toward transatlantic alignment on China; a directional read for where Western trade, tech, and defense policy is headed.
insights.quote: the ability to compete and defend will require coordinated action
insights.locator: As the implications of China's rise prove costly

insights.claim: GMF's current China analysis centers on Beijing's push in the South China Sea, with a piece on the topic published June 23, 2026 by Bonnie S. Glaser and Collin Koh.
insights.speaker: German Marshall Fund
insights.so_what: Indo-Pacific maritime tension is on the front of GMF's agenda; a marker for escalation risk in shipping lanes and regional defense spending.
insights.quote: China's Push in the South China Sea

insights.claim: GMF treats the Trump-Xi summit as a live, recent focus, publishing an 'Unpacking the Trump-Xi Summit' analysis dated May 26, 2026 by Bonnie S. Glaser and Sarah Beran.
insights.speaker: German Marshall Fund
insights.so_what: A US-China summit in 2026 is a macro event for tariffs and tech controls; flags where bilateral signaling is being read by transatlantic analysts.

insights.claim: GMF frames the war in Ukraine as a turning point that pushed the EU toward rivalry with China, per Jonas Parello-Plesner.
insights.speaker: German Marshall Fund
insights.so_what: Links Russia's war directly to a structural EU pivot against China; supports a thesis of durable, not cyclical, EU-China decoupling pressure.
insights.quote: The War in Ukraine Turns the EU toward Rivalry with China
